item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clinical tone here is really weak in several places, though that's not the main factor dragging this down in my eyes. That factor is that the payoff of this article just really isn't there in any way. There's no explanation or even subtle hints of what "Joined It!" means, and since it's intended to be the climax or reveal, a lack of any meaning leaves this article devoid of any real impact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
I find this to be a really fun idea. If my Bop It! started talking to me, I'd probably keep it a secret, unlike those civilians. I can't believe nobody thought of this sort of thing until the 3000s!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what a Bop It! is. But I really enjoyed it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to hear you enjoyed it. Thank's for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. Gotta go No-Vote on this one. I agree with the people above me that the idea is really interesting. A Bop-It that can only communicate via verbs, that’s a good idea. Though I feel like this idea wasn’t expanded enough. The Bop-It just joined the other one? This isn’t expanded upon and the ending feels like it isn’t really connected to the tone of the rest of the article. It’s good, but this just keeps it from an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, boy. I used to love Bop-Its, and this touched my nostalgic-creepy nerve in a very unexpected way.

What unsettled me more than a Bop-It communicating in a very Bop-Itty way was the looming expectation that at some point, it was going to break form and say something… un-Bop-Itty. I knew it was going to happen, and I was waiting nervously for it to happen.

When it happened, it did give me a little tingle, but it left me wanting more. I really like this, but it only feels like a quarter of a good meal, you know? Novote from me, but good job nonetheless!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback. I found it very helpful.

item.title: Doin some crit
item.content:encoded:
Cute. A few things worth noting:

collapsible for wiki mods who don't like long posts in muh chrishtun forums
– hide block

The setup to this is, admittedly, interesting. I can't say how the Foundation was able to determine that a child's toy was sentient (especially since it's only capable of expressing itself in two worth phrases), and while that does give me some pause it's not a death knell.

The larger issue I have with this is (besides the unnecessary blackboxing seriously you don't need to blackbox names and dates nobody cares) that it all feels like setup, and then there's no payoff. There's nothing narratively interesting about it - it's just a toy that the Foundation has decided is sentient. Neither of the addenda do anything to further any semblance of a plot.

The second addendum specifically breaks so far from clinical tone that it nearly gave me whiplash. These lines:

SCP-3736 is shown to be aware that it is not human and shows respect for those who care for it. It holds a great amount of love for people and shows kindness towards anyone who speaks to it.

-actually, physically caused me discomfort. They're so out of place in this format it's staggering. The rest of this:

It can differentiate between who uses it and seems to possess a photographic memory. It is unknown how this is possible due to it lacking any human senses. It is presumed that SCsP3736 holds some sort of telepathic quality that allows it to communicate with humans and recognize them.

-establishes information that the Foundation cannot possibly know. I will extend my disbelief far enough to encompass "the Foundation has figured out how to establish a test for sentience", but I cannot do the same with "the Foundation knows whether or not a toy's memory is photogenic and/or that it is telepathic." That's absolutely buck wild and there's no way the Foundation would have that information.

Then it just stops, and you're left frustrated by the lack of something worth digging your teeth into. I don't think I would downvote this (because I'll admit the dialogue is cute (even if the doctor's questions are insane things to be asking a toy, regardless of whether or not it can think)), but I certainly wouldn't upvote it. It's just not interested in doing anything more than it does, and frankly that's just not enough.

My score is in the collapsible below, but tl;dr: cute premise that suffers from you not expanding on whether or not you have to smack this thing to get it to talk isn't enough to carry a weak execution and lack of narrative intrigue.

Show djkaktus' Advanced Metric Article Score ©
Close

djkaktus' Advanced Metric Article Score

Concept: 6/10
Execution: 4/10
Presentation: 3/10

Cute concept doesn't do quite enough to be really interesting.

Overall Score: 4.3/10


SS - S - A - B - C - D - F
